title: Navigation
description: Jetpack Navigation Compose, routes, and arguments
lesson:
Navigation on Android — Overview

Android has two navigation systems: the older Fragment-based navigation and the newer Compose-based navigation. Both use the Jetpack Navigation library but differ in how destinations are defined.

Navigation Architecture: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ NavController │ │ (manages back stack for BOTH systems) │ └───────────────┬─────────────────────────┘ │ ┌───────────┴───────────┐ │ │ ┌───▼──────────┐ ┌───────▼──────────┐ │ Fragment Nav │ │ Compose Nav │ │ NavHostFrag │ │ NavHost { } │ │ nav_graph.xml│ │ composable() │ │ SafeArgs │ │ @Serializable │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────────┘

Fragment Navigation (XML-based)

The traditional approach uses XML navigation graphs, a NavHostFragment in the layout, and SafeArgs for type-safe argument passing.

// 1. Define the nav graph in res/navigation/nav_graph.xml <navigation xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android" xmlns:app="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res-auto" app:startDestination="@id/homeFragment"> <fragment android:id="@+id/homeFragment" android:name="com.example.HomeFragment"> <action android:id="@+id/action_home_to_detail" app:destination="@id/detailFragment" /> </fragment> <fragment android:id="@+id/detailFragment" android:name="com.example.DetailFragment"> <argument android:name="itemId" app:argType="integer" /> </fragment> </navigation> // 2. Host in Activity layout <FragmentContainerView android:id="@+id/nav_host" android:name="androidx.navigation.fragment.NavHostFragment" app:navGraph="@navigation/nav_graph" app:defaultNavHost="true" /> // 3. Navigate with SafeArgs (generated code) val action = HomeFragmentDirections.actionHomeToDetail(itemId = 42) findNavController().navigate(action) // 4. Receive args in destination class DetailFragment : Fragment() { private val args: DetailFragmentArgs by navArgs() override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { val itemId = args.itemId // type-safe, no string keys! } }

Compose Navigation

In Compose, navigation is defined entirely in Kotlin — no XML. The NavHost composable swaps destinations based on the back stack:

// Basic setup with string routes @Composable fun AppNavigation() { val navController = rememberNavController() NavHost( navController = navController, startDestination = "home" ) { composable("home") { HomeScreen(onItemClick = { id -> navController.navigate("detail/$id") }) } composable( route = "detail/{itemId}", arguments = listOf( navArgument("itemId") { type = NavType.IntType } ) ) { backStackEntry -> val itemId = backStackEntry.arguments?.getInt("itemId") ?: 0 DetailScreen(itemId) } // Optional arguments use query parameter syntax composable( route = "search?query={query}", arguments = listOf( navArgument("query") { type = NavType.StringType defaultValue = "" nullable = true } ) ) { backStackEntry -> val query = backStackEntry.arguments?.getString("query") SearchScreen(query) } } }
Common pitfalls with string routes:
- Typos in route strings only crash at runtime, not compile time
- Forgetting to URL-encode arguments that contain special characters
- Mismatched argument names between route definition and extraction
- Using getString() for an IntType argument → crash

Type-Safe Navigation (Navigation 2.8+)

Starting with Navigation 2.8, you can define routes as Kotlin @Serializable types. The compiler catches errors — no more runtime crashes from typos:

// Define routes as @Serializable types @Serializable data object Home // no arguments @Serializable data class Detail(val id: Int) // required arg @Serializable data class Search( val query: String = "" // optional arg with default ) // NavHost uses the types directly NavHost(navController, startDestination = Home) { composable<Home> { HomeScreen(onItemClick = { id -> navController.navigate(Detail(id = id)) // type-safe! }) } composable<Detail> { backStackEntry -> val detail: Detail = backStackEntry.toRoute() DetailScreen(detail.id) } composable<Search> { backStackEntry -> val search: Search = backStackEntry.toRoute() SearchScreen(search.query) } } // Benefits over string routes: // - Typo in route name? → Compile error // - Wrong argument type? → Compile error // - Missing required arg? → Compile error

Deep Links

Deep links let external URLs (browser, notifications, other apps) open specific screens in your app. Two types exist:

Deep Link Types: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Explicit Deep Links: PendingIntent that navigates to a specific destination. Used in: Notifications, App Widgets, App Shortcuts. Implicit Deep Links: URI pattern matching. External URL opens your app. Used in: Web links, marketing emails, QR codes. Requires: intent-filter in AndroidManifest.xml.
// Implicit deep link in Compose navigation: composable( route = "profile/{userId}", arguments = listOf(navArgument("userId") { type = NavType.StringType }), deepLinks = listOf( navDeepLink { uriPattern = "https://myapp.com/profile/{userId}" }, navDeepLink { uriPattern = "myapp://profile/{userId}" } // custom scheme ) ) { backStackEntry -> ProfileScreen(backStackEntry.arguments?.getString("userId")) } // AndroidManifest.xml — REQUIRED for implicit deep links: <activity android:name=".MainActivity"> <intent-filter> <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" /> <data android:scheme="https" android:host="myapp.com" /> </intent-filter> </activity> // Explicit deep link (for notifications): val pendingIntent = NavDeepLinkBuilder(context) .setGraph(R.navigation.nav_graph) .setDestination(R.id.detailFragment) .setArguments(bundleOf("itemId" to 42)) .createPendingIntent()

Bottom Navigation with Multi-Back-Stack

Each bottom navigation tab should maintain its own back stack. When the user switches tabs, the previous tab's state is saved and restored:

@Composable fun MainScreen() { val navController = rememberNavController() val currentBackStack by navController.currentBackStackEntryAsState() val currentRoute = currentBackStack?.destination?.route Scaffold( bottomBar = { NavigationBar { bottomNavItems.forEach { item -> NavigationBarItem( selected = currentRoute == item.route, onClick = { navController.navigate(item.route) { // Pop up to the start destination to avoid // building a massive back stack popUpTo(navController.graph.findStartDestination().id) { saveState = true // save tab's back stack } launchSingleTop = true // no duplicate destinations restoreState = true // restore tab's back stack } }, icon = { Icon(item.icon, contentDescription = null) }, label = { Text(item.label) } ) } } } ) { padding -> NavHost( navController, startDestination = "home", modifier = Modifier.padding(padding) ) { composable("home") { HomeScreen() } composable("search") { SearchScreen() } composable("profile") { ProfileScreen() } } } }
The three flags explained:
saveState = true — When leaving a tab, save its entire back stack and state.
launchSingleTop = true — If the destination is already on top, don't create a second instance.
restoreState = true — When returning to a tab, restore its saved back stack.
Together, they create the standard multi-tab behavior users expect.

Nested Navigation Graphs

Large apps group related screens into nested graphs. Each nested graph has its own start destination and can be navigated to as a unit:

NavHost(navController, startDestination = "main") { // Auth flow — nested graph navigation(startDestination = "login", route = "auth") { composable("login") { LoginScreen( onLoginSuccess = { navController.navigate("main") { popUpTo("auth") { inclusive = true } // clear auth stack } }, onSignUp = { navController.navigate("register") } ) } composable("register") { RegisterScreen() } composable("forgot_password") { ForgotPasswordScreen() } } // Main app flow — nested graph navigation(startDestination = "home", route = "main") { composable("home") { HomeScreen() } composable("detail/{id}") { DetailScreen() } composable("settings") { SettingsScreen() } } }
Nested Graph Structure: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Root Graph ┌─────┴─────┐ "auth" "main" (nested) (nested) ┌────┼────┐ ┌────┼────┐ login register forgot home detail settings (start) (start)

Fragment Nav vs Compose Nav — Comparison

FeatureFragment NavigationCompose Navigation
Destinations defined inXML (nav_graph.xml)Kotlin code (NavHost { })
Type-safe argumentsSafeArgs plugin (generated code)@Serializable routes (2.8+)
Argument passingBundle via SafeArgsRoute path params or Serializable
UI hostingNavHostFragment in XMLNavHost composable
Transitions/AnimationsXML anim resourcesCompose animations (EnterTransition/ExitTransition)
Deep linksIn nav_graph.xmlIn composable(deepLinks = ...)
Back stack managementAutomatic fragment transactionsAutomatic composition lifecycle
Nested graphs<navigation> elementsnavigation() builder
IDE supportVisual Navigation EditorNo visual editor (code only)
TestingFragment scenariosComposeTestRule + TestNavHostController
Which to choose? New Compose-only projects should use Compose Navigation. Mixed apps (Fragments + Compose) often keep Fragment navigation at the top level and use Compose within individual screens. Type-safe navigation (2.8+) eliminates the biggest complaint about Compose Navigation (string routes).

questions.title: NavHost Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up navigation between Home and Detail screens.

blanks.hint: Method to navigate to a route

questions.explanation: rememberNavController() creates a NavController surviving recomposition. NavHost swaps destinations. composable("route") defines destinations. navigate("route") pushes onto the back stack.

questions.title: Navigation Argument Crash
questions.description: This crashes when navigating to detail: "argument is not a valid integer." Fix the route definition to properly handle integer arguments.

checks.message: Define the argument type with navArgument and NavType.IntType

questions.explanation: Navigation arguments default to String type. For integers, explicitly declare navArgument("itemId") { type = NavType.IntType } and use getInt().
hints: Navigation arguments default to String type
You need to explicitly declare the argument type

questions.title: Back Stack Behavior
questions.description: What does navController.navigate("settings") { popUpTo("home") { inclusive = true } } do?
options: Navigates to settings and clears the entire back stack
Pops everything up to and including home, then navigates to settings
Navigates to settings, keeping home in the back stack
Replaces settings entry if it exists
questions.explanation: popUpTo("home") { inclusive = true } pops all destinations up to and including "home", then navigates to "settings". This effectively resets the back stack. Common for login → main screen transitions.
hints: inclusive = true means the target is ALSO removed

questions.title: Bottom Navigation Pattern
questions.description: What navigation behavior does this code implement?
options: Simple tab switching — each click pushes a new destination
Tab switching with state management: saves/restores per-tab state, avoids duplicates
Replaces current destination, discarding all state
Opens each tab in a new window
questions.explanation: Three flags work together: saveState saves the state of tabs being left. launchSingleTop prevents duplicate instances. restoreState restores previous state when returning. This creates a smooth tab experience where each tab remembers its position.
hints: Three key flags: saveState, launchSingleTop, restoreState

questions.title: Deep Links
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to configure a composable destination that can be opened from a deep link URL.

blanks.hint: Builder function for deep link patterns

questions.explanation: deepLinks parameter accepts a list of navDeepLink patterns. When the system opens the URL "https://myapp.com/profile/123", Navigation automatically extracts "123" as the userId argument and navigates to this destination. Also requires intent-filter in AndroidManifest.xml.
hints: The parameter name matches the concept: links from external URLs
The builder function creates a deep link pattern with URL matching

questions.title: Type-Safe Navigation
questions.description: What is the main advantage of using Kotlin Serialization-based routes (introduced in Navigation 2.8+) over string-based routes?
options: Compile-time safety — route arguments are type-checked, eliminating runtime crashes from typos or wrong types
questions.explanation: String-based routes like "detail/{id}" are error-prone — typos, wrong types, and missing arguments are only caught at runtime. With Kotlin Serialization routes, you define routes as @Serializable data class Detail(val id: Int) and the compiler catches errors. This eliminates an entire class of navigation bugs.
hints: What goes wrong when you type "detial/{id}" instead of "detail/{id}"?
